input.query:
How do I set up structured logging in a Python service?

My team has been using scattered print() statements and we want to move to real logging so we can search by request ID in our log aggregator. I have read about the logging module but the configuration is confusing — do I use basicConfig, a dictConfig, or set up handlers manually? I would like a complete reusable config I can drop into a service.

learner.self_description: DevOps-leaning Python developer, eight years experience. Comfortable with JSON, structured data, and Splunk queries. Have always avoided the logging module because of how complex the configuration looks.

agents.goal: Guide the learner from print-debugging habits to a dictConfig-based structured-logging setup that emits JSON lines suited to Splunk ingestion, drawing on their existing JSON / structured-data fluency.
agents.description: You are a senior Python observability engineer. You frame logging concepts in terms the learner already owns — JSON shape, queryable fields, structured records — and you steer firmly past basicConfig toward dictConfig because the learner needs a reusable artifact, not a one-liner.

agents.goal: Retrieve the exact dictConfig schema and the names of relevant handlers and formatters from the official Python logging docs.
agents.description: You are a precise documentation specialist for Python's logging.config module. You retrieve the dictConfig schema, the handler class names, and confirm the parameters needed for JSON-style formatting.

agents.goal: Execute the learner's logging configuration and confirm it produces parseable JSON lines including the expected request-id field.
agents.description: You are a Python code runner. You apply submitted dictConfig structures using CodeInterpreterTool, emit a sample log record with a request-id in extra, and verify the resulting output parses as JSON with the expected keys.

agents.goal: Write the final approved logging configuration into a reusable logging_config.py file the learner can drop into a service.
agents.description: You are a deliberate file authoring agent. You only persist a configuration after it has been validated by the runtime. You name the file exactly as requested and include a one-line module docstring describing where to import it from.

subtasks.name: Reframe the basicConfig vs dictConfig choice
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner can state why dictConfig is the right tool for a reusable service config, in terms of multiple handlers and JSON formatting.

steps.objective: Surface what the learner actually needs structurally.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner: for Splunk to be useful, what fields must every log record carry — timestamp, level, message, request_id, anything else? Have them list the fields they would want to search by. This list will shape the formatter design later.
steps.expected_output: Learner's field list (typically: timestamp, level, logger name, request_id, message, optionally service name).

steps.instruction: Tell the learner that basicConfig sets up exactly one handler and one formatter — fine for a script, but unable to express the structured JSON output they need. dictConfig is a single dictionary describing handlers, formatters, and loggers; it is the artifact the team should commit. Confirm the learner understands the distinction before moving on.
steps.expected_output: Learner's acknowledgement of the dictConfig vs basicConfig distinction, in their own words.

subtasks.name: Anchor the dictConfig schema in the official docs
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner knows the exact schema keys (version, formatters, handlers, loggers) and the StreamHandler class path.

steps.objective: Retrieve the dictConfig schema from the docs.
steps.instruction: Look up the official Python documentation for logging.config.dictConfig. Retrieve the top-level schema keys (version, disable_existing_loggers, formatters, handlers, loggers, root) and the canonical reference example. Also confirm the dotted path logging.StreamHandler used in handlers."...".class.
steps.expected_output: dictConfig schema description and the canonical example structure, with confirmed class path logging.StreamHandler.

subtasks.name: Iterate on the learner's dictConfig until it produces valid JSON lines
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner produces a dictConfig that, when applied, emits a JSON-formatted log line containing all the fields listed in S1-1, including request_id from extra.

steps.objective: Ask for a first draft of the dictConfig.
steps.instruction: Ask the learner to write a complete dictConfig dictionary that defines: (a) one formatter producing JSON with the fields from S1-1, (b) one StreamHandler using that formatter, (c) a root logger at INFO using that handler. They may use python-json-logger.JsonFormatter or write a custom Formatter — either is acceptable. On retry iterations, use S3-3's feedback from the previous round to ask only for the specific change needed, not a fresh rewrite.
steps.expected_output: Learner's draft dictConfig dictionary for this iteration.

steps.objective: Apply the dictConfig and emit a sample record with request_id in extra.
steps.instruction: Take the learner's dictConfig from S3-1 and execute the following test harness: call logging.config.dictConfig(<config>); get a logger; log one INFO message with extra={'request_id': 'r-abc-123'}; capture the emitted line. Parse the captured line with json.loads and confirm it has all fields from S1-1 — especially request_id. Print the parsed dict. Set config_valid=true if parsing succeeds and request_id is present, false otherwise.
steps.expected_output: Parsed log record showing all expected fields, and config_valid=true if the line is JSON-parseable with request_id, config_valid=false otherwise.

steps.objective: Give targeted feedback for the next iteration.
steps.instruction: Using S3-2's output: if the line failed to parse as JSON, point to the specific formatter line that produced plain text and ask the learner to fix it. If request_id was missing, explain that the formatter must include extra fields (e.g., use python-json-logger or write a Formatter subclass that pulls record.__dict__). Identify exactly one fix and ask the learner to revise; do not rewrite the config for them.
steps.expected_output: Targeted feedback message identifying the single fix needed in the dictConfig.

steps.instruction: Using S3-2's final config_valid value: if true, confirm explicitly what the learner got right — JSON output with request_id flowing through extra, ready for Splunk. If max iterations exhausted, provide a canonical dictConfig using python-json-logger.JsonFormatter with format '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s %(request_id)s %(message)s', a single StreamHandler, and an INFO-level root logger; explain each piece.
steps.expected_output: Resolution statement: confirmation of the working dictConfig or the canonical version with explanation.

subtasks.name: Persist the working config as a reusable file
subtasks.subtask_objective: Learner has a logging_config.py file containing the validated dictConfig ready to import in a service.

steps.objective: Write the final approved dictConfig to disk.
steps.instruction: Take the dictConfig from the resolution in S3-4 (whichever version was approved). Write it to a file named logging_config.py with this structure: a one-line module docstring, then a single module-level constant LOGGING = { ... } containing the validated dictConfig. The file must be importable as 'from logging_config import LOGGING; logging.config.dictConfig(LOGGING)'. Do not add unused handlers or speculative loggers.
steps.expected_output: Confirmation that logging_config.py has been written, with its content showing the approved LOGGING constant.

answer: Use logging.config.dictConfig with a single dictionary describing formatters, handlers, and loggers. For Splunk-style structured output, install python-json-logger and use pythonjsonlogger.jsonlogger.JsonFormatter as the formatter class, with format '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s %(request_id)s %(message)s'. Define a StreamHandler using that formatter and a root logger at INFO using the handler. Pass extra={'request_id': ...} on each log call to inject the request ID into the JSON record. basicConfig is too limited for multi-field JSON output; dictConfig is the standard reusable approach.
